original_hypothesis.title: CurveConnector Functions Missing Reentrancy Guard Enable Potential State Corruption
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Multiple CurveConnector functions that make external calls to Convex/Curve/Prisma protocols lack the nonReentrant modifier: depositIntoConvexBooster, withdrawFromConvexBooster, withdrawFromConvexRewardPool, withdrawFromGauge, withdrawFromPrisma. If any of these external protocols have callback mechanisms (e.g., through token hooks), the connector could be re-entered during execution, potentially corrupting internal state or double-executing operations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Other similar functions in the same contract (openCurvePosition, decreaseCurvePosition, depositIntoGauge) correctly include nonReentrant. The asymmetry suggests an oversight. Convex booster interactions involve ERC20 transfers that could trigger hooks on non-standard tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: depositIntoConvexBooster has onlyManager but no nonReentrant; makes external call to convexBooster.deposit(pid, amount, stake)

preconditions: External protocol (Convex/Curve/Prisma) has a callback mechanism during deposit/withdraw
Callback triggers re-entry into the CurveConnector or another onlyManager function
The flashLoan address or keeper can be triggered in the callback chain

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: External calls to convexBooster.deposit/withdraw without reentrancy protection
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Re-entry during deposit/withdraw could double-deposit or double-withdraw LP tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Double-withdraw from reward pools extracts twice the rewards; double-deposit could lock excess LP tokens

economic_questions: Do Convex/Curve/Prisma contracts have any callback mechanisms?
Can the flashLoan address facilitate re-entry through these protocols?
What is the maximum value extractable through a re-entry?
validation_requirements: Audit Convex Booster deposit/withdraw for callback vectors
Test reentrancy with a mock callback through onlyManager modifier's flashLoan path
Verify that re-entry into the same function causes measurable state corruption

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on the value staked in affected pools
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to the value of staked LP tokens in affected pools

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When large LP amounts are staked

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to their share of vault
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value of LP tokens in affected reward pools

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Convex Booster interface for callback vectors
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Re-entry path analysis through onlyManager modifier

validation_result.impact_summary: Five CurveConnector functions making external calls to Convex/Curve/Prisma protocols lack the nonReentrant modifier while six equivalent functions in the same contract correctly include it, creating an inconsistent security surface that could permit cross-function reentrancy.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis is confirmed: depositIntoConvexBooster, withdrawFromConvexBooster, withdrawFromConvexRewardPool, withdrawFromGauge, and withdrawFromPrisma all lack nonReentrant while depositIntoGauge, depositIntoPrisma, openCurvePosition, decreaseCurvePosition, harvestRewards, harvestPrismaRewards, and harvestConvexRewards all include it. This asymmetry has two consequences: (1) The five unguarded functions do not set the ReentrancyGuard lock, meaning that during their external calls, if a callback occurs, any function—including those WITH nonReentrant—can be called since the lock was never set. (2) Conversely, while a nonReentrant-protected function is executing (lock is set), the unguarded functions CAN still be called via a callback since they don't check the lock. The onlyManager modifier limits the entry to keeper, emergency, or registry.flashLoan(). The flashLoan address is a global address that passes onlyManager on ALL connectors and the AccountingManager, creating a plausible callback vector if the flashLoan provider is a contract like Balancer Vault that performs callbacks.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code inspection confirms the asymmetry. Functions with nonReentrant: depositIntoGauge (line ~78), depositIntoPrisma (line ~88), openCurvePosition (line ~114), decreaseCurvePosition (line ~153), harvestRewards (line ~196), harvestPrismaRewards (line ~204), harvestConvexRewards (line ~216). Functions WITHOUT nonReentrant: depositIntoConvexBooster (line ~103), withdrawFromConvexBooster (line ~171), withdrawFromConvexRewardPool (line ~178), withdrawFromGauge (line ~185), withdrawFromPrisma (line ~192). All five unguarded functions make external calls to untrusted/external protocol contracts.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: External call to convexBooster.deposit without nonReentrant, while similar function depositIntoGauge includes nonReentrant. During the external call, the ReentrancyGuard lock is NOT set, allowing cross-function reentrancy into any function.
External call to convexBooster.withdraw without nonReentrant. A callback during this call could re-enter other connector functions.

External call to IConvexBasicRewards.withdraw without nonReentrant. The second parameter 'true' triggers claiming rewards which involves additional token transfers that could serve as callback vectors.
External call to IRewardsGauge.withdraw without nonReentrant, while the symmetric depositIntoGauge function includes nonReentrant.
External call to IDepositToken.withdraw without nonReentrant, while the symmetric depositIntoPrisma function includes nonReentrant.

calling_sequence: 1. Manager (keeper/emergency/flashLoan) calls depositIntoConvexBooster - onlyManager passes, no ReentrancyGuard lock set
3. _approveOperations approves convexBooster to spend LP tokens
4. convexBooster.deposit(pid, amount, stake) - external call to Convex protocol
5. During convexBooster.deposit execution, if a callback occurs (e.g., through ERC20 transfer hooks or staking mechanism), the callback can re-enter the CurveConnector
6. Since nonReentrant lock was never set, ANY function (including nonReentrant-protected ones like openCurvePosition, harvestRewards, etc.) can be called
7. The re-entered function could modify Registry state (holding positions), trigger additional external calls, or interact with other connectors
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists because the ReentrancyGuard state variable (_status) is never set to _ENTERED during execution of the five unguarded functions. BaseConnector inherits ReentrancyGuard (BaseConnector.sol line 24: 'contract BaseConnector is NoyaGovernanceBase, IConnector, ReentrancyGuard'). The nonReentrant modifier sets _status = _ENTERED at function start and _NOT_ENTERED at end. Without this modifier, the lock is never engaged, meaning: (a) during the external call in the unguarded function, any function (guarded or unguarded) can be called; (b) during a guarded function's external call, the unguarded functions can still be called since they don't check _status.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that affect this specific issue. The dCurveConnector.ts deployment script shows the connector is deployed with convexBooster, cvx, crv, prisma addresses and standard BaseConnectorCP params.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack requires: (1) the external protocol (Convex/Curve/Prisma) has a callback mechanism during deposit/withdraw operations - Convex booster's deposit involves ERC20 transfers that could trigger hooks on non-standard tokens, and the 'stake=true' path involves additional staking operations; (2) the callback reaches an address that passes onlyManager - the flashLoan address is globally set and passes onlyManager for ALL vaults; (3) the re-entered function causes state corruption - functions like openCurvePosition modify Registry holding positions. While exploitation requires specific conditions (particularly the flashLoan vector), the code defect is real and the inconsistency is clearly an oversight given that peer functions in the same contract correctly include the guard.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The direct impact is that cross-function reentrancy protection is broken for these five functions. If the flashLoan address is a contract that performs callbacks (e.g., Balancer Vault), an attacker could potentially: (1) double-deposit LP tokens into Convex during a single transaction, (2) re-enter harvest functions during a withdraw to claim rewards twice, (3) manipulate Registry holding positions during external protocol interactions. The impact is bounded by the onlyManager access control but amplified by the flashLoan bypass in onlyManager.
recommended_mitigations: Add nonReentrant modifier to all five affected functions: depositIntoConvexBooster, withdrawFromConvexBooster, withdrawFromConvexRewardPool, withdrawFromGauge, withdrawFromPrisma
Establish a coding standard that ALL functions making external calls must include nonReentrant, enforced via CI/linting
Consider adding a reentrancy guard to the onlyManager modifier itself to provide defense-in-depth
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy CurveConnector with a mock Convex Booster that performs a callback during deposit. 2. Set flashLoan address to a contract that can relay calls. 3. Call depositIntoConvexBooster from the keeper. 4. In the mock booster's deposit, callback to the flashLoan contract. 5. From the flashLoan contract (which passes onlyManager), call harvestConvexRewards or openCurvePosition. 6. Verify both calls succeed (no reentrancy revert). 7. Compare with depositIntoGauge where the same scenario would revert due to nonReentrant.
uncertainties: Whether real Convex/Curve/Prisma contracts have exploitable callback mechanisms during deposit/withdraw operations
Whether the flashLoan address would be set to a contract that performs callbacks (e.g., Balancer Vault) vs a custom Noya contract
The exact state changes achievable through re-entry given the limited internal state in CurveConnector
additional_testing_needed: Test with mock external protocols that perform callbacks during deposit/withdraw to confirm cross-function reentrancy is possible
Audit the actual Convex Booster deposit/withdraw flow for callback vectors (ERC20 hooks, staking callbacks)
Verify if the flashLoan address in production deployments could serve as a callback relay
Test all permutations of re-entry between guarded and unguarded functions
